metadata.title: The Planet That Could Host a Hundred Million Species
metadata.color_semantics: TEAL = optimized parameters / high biodiversity; CRIMSON = Earth's comfortable but sub-optimal values
metadata.note: Exclusions honored: no plate tectonics recycling, no axial tilt / lunar influence, no intelligent life emergence. Wonder register.
_variant_todo: rewrite every beat narration_text in the Teardown register (voices/teardown/VOICE.md + CLAUDE-BRAND.md) — voice only, facts unchanged
signature tangent 0-1 per video, ONLY on a clear opportunity (see SKILL)
swap the outro to the Claude outro from AUTHOR.MD :: NikBearBrown
then build audience-namespaced (audio in the new voice, scenes in the new palette)

skin_warnings: B00: palette=claude but the cold open is 'un-annotated' — COLD OPEN LAW wants ClaudeComposerAsk
B11: palette=claude but the outro is 'un-annotated' — OUTRO LAW wants ClaudeTitleOutro

beats.narration_text: This is Liam, in for Bear. med davy A-I. From Sanskrit — meaning intelligent, or intellectually brilliant. The perfect name for our A-I-powered intelligent learning systems.

props.headline: Medhavy AI
props.body:
मेधावी (Medhavy): From Sanskrit, meaning
"intelligent" or "intellectually brilliant"
— the perfect name for our AI-powered
intelligent learning systems.

beats.narration_text: Earth is our only example of a living world — yet most of it is nearly barren. On every dial that matters, Earth is comfortable but never optimal. A world built for life would look nothing like Earth.

card.copy: The Planet That Could Host a Hundred Million Species

build.suggested: suggested prompt: Earth is our only example of a living world — yet most of it is nearly barren. On every dial that matters, Earth is comfortable but never optimal. A world built

beats.narration_text: Species richness — the number of distinct species — correlates with ecosystem boundary length: coastlines, reef edges, forest margins. Boundaries are where ecosystems meet and diversity spikes. Earth has vast dead interiors — continents — that minimize boundary per unit area.

production_viz.label: Earth map: vast continent interiors (CRIMSON=barren) vs coastlines (TEAL=diverse); species richness at boundaries

beats.narration_text: Imagine an archipelago world: no large continents, only scattered islands surrounded by shallow sea. Coastline 45 times longer than Earth's. Every ocean patch is near a reef or island edge. The entire ocean is in the productive coastal zone — not just 7% of it.

card.copy: What would an optimized living world look like?
card.sub: 45× more coastline — every ocean patch in the productive zone

build.suggested: suggested prompt: Imagine an archipelago world: no large continents, only scattered islands surrounded by shallow sea. Coastline 45 times longer than Earth's. Every ocean patch i

beats.narration_text: Earth's coastal and shallow-water zones cover about 7% of ocean but hold more than 50% of marine species. A fully shallow archipelago world has its entire ocean in that productive zone. Conservative estimate: 5 to 10 times more marine biodiversity alone.

production_viz.label: Earth: 7% coastal zone, 50%+ species. Archipelago: 100% coastal zone, 5-10x more species. TEAL=productive, CRIMSON=barren

beats.narration_text: Star choice matters. Our Sun provides stable energy for about 5 billion more years — comfortable. An orange dwarf — K-type — provides stable energy for 70 billion years. Life on Earth took about 4 billion years to reach multicellular complexity. A 70-gigayear host star multiplies the evolutionary time budget 14-fold.

production_viz.label: Timeline: Sun 5 Gyr remaining (CRIMSON=limited) vs K-dwarf 70 Gyr (TEAL=abundant); 14x evolutionary budget

beats.narration_text: On the archipelago world orbiting an orange dwarf: total species perhaps 100 million versus Earth's roughly 9 million. More coastline, more evolutionary time, more stable radiation environment. Each parameter separately helps; together they multiply.

production_viz.label: Species counter: Earth 9M vs optimal planet 100M; parameter dials: coastline×45, star life×14; TEAL=optimized

beats.narration_text: Earth's large continents create vast interior deserts — the Sahara, the Australian outback, the Siberian interior — where biodiversity is sparse. An archipelago world eliminates interiors. Every point of land is within a few kilometers of the sea. The desert zone is eliminated.

production_viz.label: Earth: Sahara/Australia/Siberia highlighted as barren (CRIMSON). Archipelago: all land near sea (TEAL). Desert zone = 0.

beats.narration_text: The formula S proportional to E — species-energy and species-area relationships — is empirically established for Earth: islands with more coastline and energy input support exponentially more species. Extrapolating to an optimized planet is speculative but grounded in this same relationship.

production_viz.label: S ∝ E curve; island data points fitting relationship; extrapolation to archipelago planet; TEAL fit line

beats.narration_text: This is not just science fiction. As astronomers catalog exoplanets, they identify candidates matching this description: K-dwarf host stars, moderate sizes, likely ocean worlds with island geography. The Drake equation becomes: not just "is there life?" but "how much?"

production_viz.label: Exoplanet catalog; K-dwarf candidates highlighted; ocean world icon; 'how much life?' question; TEAL=promising candidates

beats.narration_text: More coastline means more ecosystem boundary means more species. Longer-lived star means more evolutionary time means more complexity. Shallow global ocean means productive everywhere. 100 million species instead of 9 million. Earth is comfortable. Optimal looks different.

card.sub: 45× more coastline + 70 Gyr star + shallow global ocean

build.suggested: suggested prompt: More coastline means more ecosystem boundary means more species. Longer-lived star means more evolutionary time means more complexity. Shallow global ocean mean

beats.narration_text: med davy. A-I-powered intelligent learning systems. On YouTube, at med davy A-I. med davy dot com.
